IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper provides a valuable resource (a 1,200‑sentence Singlish UD treebank) and demonstrates substantial empirical gains from neural stacking (≈36.5% relative error reduction; ~84.2% UAS) which will be useful for low‑resource and code‑switched parsing work. Code release and clear hyperparameter reporting improve reproducibility and likelihood of reuse. However, the dataset is small and the contributions are primarily an application of known transfer techniques to a niche variety, so the work is unlikely to fundamentally shift parsing paradigms though it opens practical avenues for Singlish and similar low‑resource varieties. | B(4): This paper tackles an important and under-resourced problem by providing a Singlish UD treebank (1,200 sentences) and demonstrating large empirical gains from feature-level neural stacking (36.5% relative error reduction in parsing; ~31.6% in POS). The resource plus the transfer approach materially advance practical cross-lingual transfer for non-standard, contact-variety English and will enable follow-up work on annotation, analysis of Singlish-specific phenomena, and improved downstream systems. While the dataset size is modest, the combination of a publicly released treebank and clear, large improvements makes the contribution likely to be widely used and to open meaningful new research directions for low-resource/variety adaptation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides a valuable resource (a 1,200‑sentence Singlish UD treebank) and demonstrates substantial empirical gains from neural stacking (≈36.5% relative error reduction; ~84.2% UAS) which will be useful for low‑resource and code‑switched parsing work. Code release and clear hyperparameter reporting improve reproducibility and likelihood of reuse. However, the dataset is small and the contributions are primarily an application of known transfer techniques to a niche variety, so the work is unlikely to fundamentally shift parsing paradigms though it opens practical avenues for Singlish and similar low‑resource varieties.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important and under-resourced problem by providing a Singlish UD treebank (1,200 sentences) and demonstrating large empirical gains from feature-level neural stacking (36.5% relative error reduction in parsing; ~31.6% in POS). The resource plus the transfer approach materially advance practical cross-lingual transfer for non-standard, contact-variety English and will enable follow-up work on annotation, analysis of Singlish-specific phenomena, and improved downstream systems. While the dataset size is modest, the combination of a publicly released treebank and clear, large improvements makes the contribution likely to be widely used and to open meaningful new research directions for low-resource/variety adaptation.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper contributes a novel resource (a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank) and demonstrates strong empirical gains by applying feature-level neural stacking from English models to Singlish parsing and tagging. Methodologically, however, the modelling components (biaffine parser, biLSTM-CRF tagger, and neural stacking for transfer) are established techniques, so the work is an incremental application of existing methods to a new low-resource variety rather than a new algorithmic idea. The authors appear to have clearly separated their resource contribution from prior work and quantified transfer benefits, which would be informative to practitioners but unlikely to surprise experts seeking fundamentally new methods. | B(3): The paper's main novelty lies in the new Singlish UD dependency treebank (1,200 sentences), which is a useful resource for a low-resource, typologically-mixed variety and not previously available. Methodologically it applies existing neural stacking and biaffine parsing techniques to transfer English syntactic knowledge; this is a sensible and well-executed application but not a fundamentally new algorithmic advance. The work clearly differentiates its contributions (resource + adaptation experiments) and demonstrates notable empirical gains, so it is an incremental but meaningful contribution rather than a highly original methodological breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper contributes a novel resource (a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank) and demonstrates strong empirical gains by applying feature-level neural stacking from English models to Singlish parsing and tagging. Methodologically, however, the modelling components (biaffine parser, biLSTM-CRF tagger, and neural stacking for transfer) are established techniques, so the work is an incremental application of existing methods to a new low-resource variety rather than a new algorithmic idea. The authors appear to have clearly separated their resource contribution from prior work and quantified transfer benefits, which would be informative to practitioners but unlikely to surprise experts seeking fundamentally new methods.

Methodologically it applies existing neural stacking and biaffine parsing techniques to transfer English syntactic knowledge; this is a sensible and well-executed application but not a fundamentally new algorithmic advance. The work clearly differentiates its contributions (resource + adaptation experiments) and demonstrates notable empirical gains, so it is an incremental but meaningful contribution rather than a highly original methodological breakthrough.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This work—creating a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank and demonstrating substantial parser and POS gains via neural stacking from UD English—directly addresses core interests of top NLP venues (low-resource parsing, cross-lingual transfer, dataset release). The experimental setup uses community-standard metrics (UAS/LAS, POS accuracy), reports hyperparameters, and the authors release code, which aligns with the reproducibility and engineering concerns of the audience. While the treebank is small, the paper’s focus on transfer learning methods and clear empirical gains (large relative error reductions) makes it highly relevant to the conference readership. | B(5): This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP venue scope: it presents a new UD-compliant Singlish dependency treebank (1,200 sentences) and demonstrates significant cross-lingual transfer gains via feature-level neural stacking (36.5% relative error reduction for parsing, 31.6% for POS). The methods and evaluation (UAS/LAS, POS accuracy), comparisons to English baselines (UD-Eng) and release of code meet community standards, and the work will be of clear interest to researchers in low-resource parsing, cross-lingual transfer, and syntactic annotation projects.

reviewer_a.justification: This work—creating a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank and demonstrating substantial parser and POS gains via neural stacking from UD English—directly addresses core interests of top NLP venues (low-resource parsing, cross-lingual transfer, dataset release). The experimental setup uses community-standard metrics (UAS/LAS, POS accuracy), reports hyperparameters, and the authors release code, which aligns with the reproducibility and engineering concerns of the audience. While the treebank is small, the paper’s focus on transfer learning methods and clear empirical gains (large relative error reductions) makes it highly relevant to the conference readership.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP venue scope: it presents a new UD-compliant Singlish dependency treebank (1,200 sentences) and demonstrates significant cross-lingual transfer gains via feature-level neural stacking (36.5% relative error reduction for parsing, 31.6% for POS). The methods and evaluation (UAS/LAS, POS accuracy), comparisons to English baselines (UD-Eng) and release of code meet community standards, and the work will be of clear interest to researchers in low-resource parsing, cross-lingual transfer, and syntactic annotation projects.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized with a clear flow from dataset construction to models and experiments, and the main contributions (Singlish UD treebank and neural-stacking transfer for POS/parsing) are stated early. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and hyperparameters are reported, aiding reproducibility; figures/tables and examples are informative. Writing quality is good overall, though a few presentation choices (e.g. clearer discussion of the coarse vs. fine-grained POS decisions and more explicit pointers to specific tables/ablation results) could be improved. From a presentation standpoint this is a solid paper with only minor clarity issues. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear progression from dataset creation to models and experiments, and the main contributions (the 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank and the neural stacking improvements to POS tagging and parsing) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables/examples are informative for the experimental claims (UAS/LAS and POS accuracy improvements). Minor presentation quibbles remain—e.g., the authors note differences due to not using fine-grained POS tags for the English parser—that could be clarified further, but overall the writing is good and largely unambiguous.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized with a clear flow from dataset construction to models and experiments, and the main contributions (Singlish UD treebank and neural-stacking transfer for POS/parsing) are stated early. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and hyperparameters are reported, aiding reproducibility; figures/tables and examples are informative. Writing quality is good overall, though a few presentation choices (e.g. clearer discussion of the coarse vs. fine-grained POS decisions and more explicit pointers to specific tables/ablation results) could be improved. From a presentation standpoint this is a solid paper with only minor clarity issues.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear progression from dataset creation to models and experiments, and the main contributions (the 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank and the neural stacking improvements to POS tagging and parsing) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables/examples are informative for the experimental claims (UAS/LAS and POS accuracy improvements). Minor presentation quibbles remain—e.g., the authors note differences due to not using fine-grained POS tags for the English parser—that could be clarified further, but overall the writing is good and largely unambiguous.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents solid experiments supporting its claims: a new 1,200‑sentence Singlish UD treebank, baseline Singlish and English parsers, and clear improvements from feature‑level neural stacking (reported as large relative error reductions for UAS and POS). Reproducibility is aided by released code and reported hyperparameters, and limitations (small training data) are acknowledged. However, the summary lacks evidence of multiple runs/error bars, detailed split/seed reporting and some diagnostic ablations or oracle upper bounds (e.g., impact of POS errors on parsing) that I would expect for full rigor. Overall the experimental depth is strong but missing a few robustness and diagnostic analyses. | B(4): The paper presents substantial empirical evidence: a new 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank, POS and biaffine parsers, and strong transfer gains via feature-level neural stacking (36.51% relative error reduction for parsing, 31.58% for POS). Reproducibility is well supported (hyperparameters reported, code released) and related work and limitations (small Singlish training set; POS tag granularity differences) are acknowledged. However, from the summary it is unclear whether deeper ablations or sensitivity analyses (e.g., varying amounts of Singlish data, alternative transfer configurations, error analysis by construction types) were performed, leaving some important methodological questions only partially explored.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents solid experiments supporting its claims: a new 1,200‑sentence Singlish UD treebank, baseline Singlish and English parsers, and clear improvements from feature‑level neural stacking (reported as large relative error reductions for UAS and POS). Reproducibility is aided by released code and reported hyperparameters, and limitations (small training data) are acknowledged. However, the summary lacks evidence of multiple runs/error bars, detailed split/seed reporting and some diagnostic ablations or oracle upper bounds (e.g., impact of POS errors on parsing) that I would expect for full rigor. Overall the experimental depth is strong but missing a few robustness and diagnostic analyses.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents substantial empirical evidence: a new 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank, POS and biaffine parsers, and strong transfer gains via feature-level neural stacking (36.51% relative error reduction for parsing, 31.58% for POS). Reproducibility is well supported (hyperparameters reported, code released) and related work and limitations (small Singlish training set; POS tag granularity differences) are acknowledged. However, from the summary it is unclear whether deeper ablations or sensitivity analyses (e.g., varying amounts of Singlish data, alternative transfer configurations, error analysis by construction types) were performed, leaving some important methodological questions only partially explored.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates against sensible baselines (a Singlish-only model and an English UD-trained model) and reports standard metrics (UAS/LAS, POS accuracy) with hyperparameters and code released, which supports fair comparisons. However, from the summary there is no evidence they compare to other recent cross-lingual transfer or multilingual/adapter-style baselines, nor is it clear whether oracle/upper-bound or comprehensive ablation studies were run to isolate the contribution of neural stacking versus other factors. Overall the experimental comparisons are strong and appropriate for the core claim but omit some relevant competing approaches and diagnostic baselines that would make the evaluation fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper evaluates its neural-stacking approach against sensible and relevant baselines (a Singlish-only model and an English UD-trained model) using standard metrics (UAS/LAS and POS accuracy) on the same Singlish test data, and it reports hyperparameters and limitations (e.g. POS tag granularity) transparently. From a cross-lingual transfer perspective the comparisons are fair and the authors acknowledge when English-trained models differ; however, the study omits some modern transfer/representation baselines (e.g. multilingual contextual encoders or other cross-lingual transfer techniques) that are now common for low-resource parsing. Overall the empirical comparison is solid but not fully comprehensive relative to the current state of the art.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates against sensible baselines (a Singlish-only model and an English UD-trained model) and reports standard metrics (UAS/LAS, POS accuracy) with hyperparameters and code released, which supports fair comparisons. However, from the summary there is no evidence they compare to other recent cross-lingual transfer or multilingual/adapter-style baselines, nor is it clear whether oracle/upper-bound or comprehensive ablation studies were run to isolate the contribution of neural stacking versus other factors. Overall the experimental comparisons are strong and appropriate for the core claim but omit some relevant competing approaches and diagnostic baselines that would make the evaluation fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates its neural-stacking approach against sensible and relevant baselines (a Singlish-only model and an English UD-trained model) using standard metrics (UAS/LAS and POS accuracy) on the same Singlish test data, and it reports hyperparameters and limitations (e.g. POS tag granularity) transparently. From a cross-lingual transfer perspective the comparisons are fair and the authors acknowledge when English-trained models differ; however, the study omits some modern transfer/representation baselines (e.g. multilingual contextual encoders or other cross-lingual transfer techniques) that are now common for low-resource parsing. Overall the empirical comparison is solid but not fully comprehensive relative to the current state of the art.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper makes clear, testable claims (creation of a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank and improved POS/parsing performance via neural stacking) and supports them with experiments showing large relative error reductions and absolute UAS/POS scores, with code and hyperparameters reported. However, the Singlish training set is small (1,200 sentences) which raises concerns about robustness, and the summary does not report variability across runs or statistical significance tests nor detailed ablations isolating each transfer component—limits that prevent full confidence in the generality of the gains. There is no indication of obvious mathematical errors, and reasonable baselines (Singlish-only and English-trained models) are used, so the main conclusions are plausibly justified but would be stronger with significance testing, robustness analysis, and more extensive ablations. | B(4): The paper's main claims—that a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank was created and that neural stacking from English models yields large gains for both parsing (≈84.2% UAS; 36.5% relative error reduction) and POS tagging (84.79% accuracy; 31.6% relative error reduction)—are supported by concrete experiments and reported metrics, with code and hyperparameters provided. There are no mathematical proofs to check, and the experimental setup appears reasonable (Singlish baseline, English baseline, and transfer via neural stacking), but the summary does not report statistical significance testing and notes a potential mismatch in POS granularity for the English parser, which are minor methodological gaps. Overall the results as presented justify the conclusions, though reporting of significance and more analysis of annotation coverage would strengthen the technical case.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper makes clear, testable claims (creation of a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank and improved POS/parsing performance via neural stacking) and supports them with experiments showing large relative error reductions and absolute UAS/POS scores, with code and hyperparameters reported. However, the Singlish training set is small (1,200 sentences) which raises concerns about robustness, and the summary does not report variability across runs or statistical significance tests nor detailed ablations isolating each transfer component—limits that prevent full confidence in the generality of the gains. There is no indication of obvious mathematical errors, and reasonable baselines (Singlish-only and English-trained models) are used, so the main conclusions are plausibly justified but would be stronger with significance testing, robustness analysis, and more extensive ablations.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims—that a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank was created and that neural stacking from English models yields large gains for both parsing (≈84.2% UAS; 36.5% relative error reduction) and POS tagging (84.79% accuracy; 31.6% relative error reduction)—are supported by concrete experiments and reported metrics, with code and hyperparameters provided. There are no mathematical proofs to check, and the experimental setup appears reasonable (Singlish baseline, English baseline, and transfer via neural stacking), but the summary does not report statistical significance testing and notes a potential mismatch in POS granularity for the English parser, which are minor methodological gaps. Overall the results as presented justify the conclusions, though reporting of significance and more analysis of annotation coverage would strengthen the technical case.

main_claims: Created a Singlish dependency treebank of 1,200 sentences annotated under the Universal Dependencies scheme.

Trained a Singlish dependency parser based on the biaffine attention parser (Dozat & Manning, 2016) and improved it via neural stacking to transfer English syntactic knowledge, yielding large gains (36.51% relative error reduction) and ~84.21% UAS (~85% accuracy reported).

Trained a Singlish POS tagger using a bi-LSTM-CRF with feature-level neural stacking from an English POS model, improving POS tagging accuracy for Singlish to 84.79% (31.58% relative error reduction over the baseline Singlish model).
_summary.methodology: Built a Singlish dependency treebank and trained neural models: a bi-LSTM-CRF POS tagger and a biaffine-attention graph-based dependency parser, and applied feature-level neural stacking to incorporate representations from English models (trained on UD-Eng) into the Singlish tagger and parser.
datasets: ICE-SIN (Singapore component of the International Corpus of English)
baselines: Base Singlish parser (trained on Singlish treebank)
Base English parser trained on UD-Eng
limitations: Singlish training data is limited, causing poor performance when training POS tagger on Singlish alone.
Authors did not use fine-grained POS tags for the English parser, which they note causes some difference versus prior work.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - dependency parsing and cross-lingual/transfer learning

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a decade of experience building and evaluating NLP systems in both research and production; has led reproducibility audits and developed evaluation suites for dependency parsing and sequence labeling.
traits: insists on clear experimental protocols, including dataset splits, hyperparameter tuning procedures, and random seed reporting
looks for strong and well-implemented baselines, including oracle/upper-bound comparisons and ablation studies to justify each modelling choice
evaluates statistical significance and robustness (variability across runs, error bars), and checks for potential data leakage or annotation artifacts
values reproducibility artifacts: code release, model checkpoints, and detailed preprocessing/tokenization steps
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on the soundness and reproducibility of experiments, demanding detailed comparisons and diagnostics to support empirical claims.

reviewer_b.name: Cross-lingual Syntax & Impact Specialist
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor researching cross-lingual transfer, low-resource NLP, and syntactic annotation projects; has published treebanks and transfer methods for typologically diverse languages.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior cross-lingual transfer and treebanking efforts, and how the work advances the state of the art conceptually
cares about linguistic validity of annotations and whether the treebank captures relevant phenomena for the target variety
evaluates broader impact: utility to downstream tasks, community value of the resource, and suitability of transfer methods for similar low-resource scenarios
notices whether linguistic analysis (error analysis by construction types, POS mismatches, unique phenomena) is adequate to justify claims about model improvements
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates the paper's significance and novelty in cross-lingual and low-resource NLP, emphasizing linguistic soundness and the practical value of the resource and modeling contributions.
